observations.recommendation:
1.7 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as Head of the Agency, assign
an adequate number of employees to the Inventory Committee to ensure the
completion of the physical inventory within three months or less.

1.8 We reiterate our prior years’ audit recommendations, with modifications, that
the Municipal Accountant:

a. Maintain complete and updated PPE Ledger Cards to ensure proper
accounting for all PPEs and facilitate easy reconciliation of the Re-port on
the Physical Count of Property, Plant and Equipment (RPCPPE) with
accounting records;

b. Trace back the transactions related to the PPE balance recorded as
“For Update” from prior years to determine their nature and prepare the
appropriate adjustments in the books of the Municipality;

c. Record the PPEs transferred from Trust Fund books into the General
Fund (Table 2); and

d. Determine the cost of various donated equipment and recognize them
as PPE in the books of account (Table 3).

1.9 We further recommended that the Inventory Committee develop a
plan/strategy to conduct and complete the physical inventory and RPCPPE
within the prescribed period of three months or less.

2.10 We reiterated our prior years’ recommendation, with modifications, that the
Municipal Accountant:

a. Prepare the necessary journal entries to record all projects completed
in CY 2024 to their respective asset accounts and calculate the corresponding
depreciation expenses for these classified completed projects from their
completion dates until December 31, 2024;

b. Coordinate with the Municipal Engineer to reconcile the accounting
records with the Statement of Work Accomplished (SWA) provided by the
engineering office to determine the corrected total cost of the ARCP II
Farm-to-Market Road project (Table 5); and

c. Ensure the reclassification of all completed projects currently recorded
in the Construction in Progress account to their proper asset accounts.

2.11 We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer furnish the accounting
unit with copies of the validated Certificates of Completion for all completed
projects. These certificates will serve as the basis for the Municipal Accountant
in the reclassification of the CIP accounts to the appropriate proper Property,
Plant, and Equipment account.

3.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor initiate the creation of a General
Service Office (GSO) position to maintain up-to-date records and conduct
inventories of all local roads and their components.

3.7 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant maintain subsidiary
records for each road network, detailing roads and their components, to
ensure accurate accounting of the Road Network account and facilitate
reconciliation with physical count reports.

3.8 Furthermore, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer provide the
Municipal Accountant and the GSO (once created) with complete descriptions
and cost segregations of road components for all road projects to enhance
record accuracy and financial reporting.

4.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the Municipal Peace
and Order Council:

a. Submit written justification explaining why the appropriated Confidential
Funds for CY 2024 were not utilized for the expenditures detailed in
Table 6;

b. Instruct the Municipal Budget Officer to include funding for Peace and
Order (POP) in the Annual Appropriation Ordinance funds, as mandated by
Section 4.1.3 of COA-DBM-DILG-GCG-DND Joint Circular No. 2015-01,
ensuring that the total amount for Confidential Funds (CF) does not exceed
thirty percent (30%) of the LGU’s total annual POP allocation;

c. Either assume direct responsibility as the Special Disbursing Officer (SDO)
for Confidential Funds or designate a regular/permanent employee to fulfill
this function;

d. Approve all cash advances for CF and actively oversee their agency-wide
utilization and disposition; and

e. Establish and maintain stringent internal control measures to deter and
prevent irregular, unnecessary, excessive, extravagant and unconscionable
expenditures, while promoting the prudent use of government resources
for intelligence and confidential operations.

4.12 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, for future Confidential
Fund cash advances:

a. Ensure that Confidential Expenses (CE) will not exceed the authorized
funds provided for CF;

b. Record CF cash advances and maintain separate subsidiary records per
SDO;

c. Submit all DVs for cash advance for intelligence/confidential activities
with complete documentation in a sealed, labeled ("CONFIDENTIAL- For
the COA ATL Only") envelope to COA ATL within seven (7) working days
from check release, with a covering transmittal letter enumerating the
submissions;

d. Record liquidations within five days after the receipt of the copy of the
transmittal of documents duly acknowledged by the ICFAU. He shall
submit the corresponding Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to the COA ATL;
and

e. Record all the transactions using the appropriate accounts provided in the
Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs for accurate financial reporting.

5.5 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant draw an adjusting entry to
correct the misclassification of accounts and henceforth, record all future
transactions using the appropriate accounts specified in the Revised Chart of
Accounts for LGUs to ensure accurate and reliable financial reporting for all
users.

6.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a. Maintain the current practice of preparing timely and accurate bank
reconciliation statements and continue to identify and address any
discrepancies;

b. Effect the necessary adjustments immediately for the valid reconciling
item, a Credit Memo (ON-US check deposit) amounting to ₱70,957.89,
which remained unadjusted as of year-end.

c. Prepare the required adjusting entry to reinstate the cash corresponding to
the stale check amounting to ₱10,000.00 and re-establish the associated
payable account. This will prevent understatements of both assets and
obligations in the accounting records; and

d. Record the deposit of ₱122,131.94 in both the Subsidiary Ledger and
General Ledger under the Cash – Local Treasury account to ensure
accurate reflection of the account’s correct balance.

7.11 We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Mayor, as Chairman
of the Municipal Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer, identify and include in the AIP priority projects which
are ready for implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimal
utilization of the 20% DF.

7.12 We also reiterated our CY 2023 audit recommendations with modifications,
that the Municipal Engineer:

a. Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects before their inclusion in
the AIP, which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with
detailed Estimates and plans/specifications; and

b. Submit the Program of Works with the Detailed Estimates,
plans/specifications and Purchase Request to the Bids and Awards
Committee within the timeline specified in the AIP. Consider the time
required for the procurement process and project implementation to
ensure completion of all the projects within the allocated period.

8.9 We recommended that the Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer:

a. Submit a written justification for the implementation of projects (Items 1
and 2 in Table 13) despite their non-inclusion in the approved AIP;

b. Demand justification letters from the concerned Punong Barangays
pertaining to implementation of projects not in accordance with the
Memoranda of Agreement (MOA) entered into with the LGU;

c. Conduct detailed engineering of all projects, with the assistance of the
Municipal Engineer, prior to its inclusion in the AIP which includes the
preparation of the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates and
plans/specifications to facilitate thorough planning and monitoring of
40

the projects’ implementation;

d. Ensure that all PPAs implemented are included in the approved Annual
Investment Plan of the municipality; and

e. Stop the utilization of the 20% Development Fund for expenditures
that are expressly not allowed in Section 3.2.5 of DBM-DOF-DILG JMC
No. 2020-1.

9.9 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:

a. Record to the Due to other funds and Trust Liability- DRRM accounts the
CY 2024 unexpended 30% QRF totaling ₱2,307,241.01; and

b. Henceforth, strictly adhere to the provision of COA Circular No. 2012-002
on the transfer of the unutilized QRF and 70% MF DRRM-MOOE from the
General Fund to Special Trust Fund, to avoid possible misuse of DRRM
Funds.

9.10 We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant, MDRRM Officer, and
Municipal Budget Officer immediately reconcile the discrepancies between the
RAO and the monthly utilization report. A detailed review should be
conducted to ensure that all transactions are accurately recorded in both
reports.

10.11 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the Municipal DRRM
Council, together with the Municipal DRRM Officer and Municipal Budget
Officer:

a. Submit a written justification for the ₱76,618.27 overspending on rescue
vehicle maintenance, which exceeded the approved budget. This is without
prejudice to the issuance of Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance, if
warranted;

b. Implement strict monitoring of LDRRM Fund utilization and disbursements
to ensure that they do not exceed the approved budget appropriation; and

c. Avoid using LDRRM Fund for non-disaster risk reduction and management
expenses.

10.12 We also recommended that the MDRRM Officer submit a detailed budget
proposal for MDRRM Office maintenance, operations, and capital outlay to
ensure effective office functioning.

11.12 We recommended that the MDRRM Officer and Municipal Budget Officer:

a. Include all valid unpaid obligations when identifying PPAs to be charged
to the LDRRMF (Special Trust Fund) in the ensuing years;

b. Utilize the LDRRM Fund, especially those in the Special Trust Fund, for
programs, projects, and activities that align with the approved LDRRMF
Investment Plan. This ensures well-planned and MDRRM Council-authorized
fund utilization; and

c. Verify fund charging to ensure expenditures designated for the STF are
correctly charged, adhering to the approved budget and LDRRMF
Investment Plan

11.13 We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant ensure all valid
claims against appropriations are recorded in the fiscal year in which the
expenses were incurred. Additionally, payables for any unpaid year-end
expenditures should be recognized, to prevent disbursements without the
corresponding funds or budget allocations.

12.6 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Bids and Awards
Committee, thru the BAC Chairman, provide within five (5) days satisfactory
explanations and/or submit all missing documentation to substantiate the
propriety of the contracts and validity of payments. This is without prejudice
to the issuance of Notice of Suspension and/or Disallowance, if warranted.

12.7 We also recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee strictly adhere to
the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184, as well as all applicable laws,
rules, regulations, COA Circulars, and latest GPPB Resolutions, to ensure that
all awarded contracts are supported by complete and appropriate
documentation.

12.8 Lastly, we recommended that the Municipal Engineer ensure that
Occupational Safety and Health is incorporated as a distinct pay item in every
infrastructure project, in compliance with DPWH D.O. No. 56 s. 2005 dated
April 20, 2005 and actively monitor the program’s implementation to confirm
adherence to DOLE requirements and ensure adequate protection of workers
against occupational hazards.

13.8 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:

a. Ensure that essential LGU properties are covered under the General
Insurance Fund (GIF) of the Government Service Insurance System (GSIS), in
compliance with R.A. No. 656 and the guidelines outlined in COA Circular
2018-002;

b. In coordination with the Supply Officer and Municipal Budget Officer,
review the GSIS premium calculation and explore options for negotiation or
alternative coverage strategies to achieve broader insurance protection within
budget constraints; and

c. In coordination with the Municipal Budget Officer, prioritize and allocate
sufficient funds in future budgets to ensure comprehensive property insurance
coverage.

13.9 In the future application of insurance with GSIS GIF, we also recommended
that the OIC Supply Officer prepare and submit the PIF to GSIS not later than
April 30 of each year. Furnish copy of the PIF and proof of submission to GSIS
to the COA Audit Team for evaluation of the actions taken.

14.8 We recommended that the Municipal Accountant immediately compute the
correct tax liability for each affected employee and official, and reflect the
corresponding over-withheld amount as a tax refund in a payroll adjustment.

14.9 We also recommended that management, thru the Municipal Accountant,
allow regular training to relevant staff on current tax laws and BIR
pronouncements to ensure ongoing compliance and prevent future instances
of over-withholding.

15.6 We recommended that the Chairperson of the GAD Focal Point System, in
coordination with the Municipal Planning and Development Office, establish
the LGU’s GAD Database for gender analysis in the preparation of the LGU’s
GAD plan to be able to come up with gender-responsive programs that
effectively address current and relevant gender-related issues.

16.8 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and all government officials and
employees involved:

a) Submit the missing supporting documents listed in Appendix D.4 and the
required post-training reports for the travels mentioned in Appendix D.6 to
prevent potential audit suspensions by April 30, 2025;

b) Refund the excess amounts claimed for Daily Travel Expense, totaling
₱216,642.98, as detailed in Appendix D.5 by May 15, 2025; and

c) In the future, approve travel requests only when they are deemed
absolutely necessary and essential for the effective performance of an
official or employee’s mandated duties and functions.

16.9 We further recommended that management cease granting travel allowances
that exceed the limits stipulated Executive Order No. 77, to avoid the
recurrence of irregular expenditures and potential disallowances during
audits.

17.5 We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and the Head of the Human
Resources Management Office formulate and implement measures to address
the delayed release of wages for Job Order (JO) personnel. This includes
setting clear deadlines for the submission of Disbursement Vouchers and
payrolls, as well as calling the attention of concerned signatories responsible
for the delays to ensure timely processing of payments.

We reiterated our prior years’ audit recommendations with modification, that
the Municipal Accountant t:
a. Maintain a complete and updated PPE Ledger Cards to ensure that all
PPEs are properly accounted for and to easily facilitate the
reconciliation of the RPCPPE with accounting records;
b. Trace back the transactions in the PPE balance recorded in bulk from
prior years to determine its nature and prepare the appropriate
adjustments in the books of the Municipality;
c. Reclassify as inventories the items with acquisition costs that are below
the capitalization threshold of ₱15,000.00 (Table 3);
d. Reclassify the incorrectly recorded PPEs to their proper account
(Table 4);
e. Determine the cost of various donated equipment and recognize them
as PPE in the books; and
f. Coordinate with the Supply Officer and compare the latest Report on
the RPCPPE/Inventory Report with the PPELCs prior to the conduct
of physical count/inventory.

We further recommended that the Supply Officer:
a. Prepare an Inventory Custodian Slip for items below the capitalization
threshold in lieu of the previously issued Property Acknowledgment
Receipt; and
b. Submit to the Municipal Accountant the documents of the donated
properties cited in Table 5.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendations with modification that
the Municipal Accountant:
a. Continue the conduct of verification, analysis, and validation of the
balances of the Due to NGAs accounts, determine the source agencies
and the status of programs, projects and activities for which funds were
received and effect the necessary adjustments, as warranted;
b. file the request for authority to write off the unsubstantiated and/or
long outstanding balances on the Due to NGA account; and
c. Disclose in the Notes to FS the information regarding the return of the
unexpended balance of the fund transfers for Salintubig Phase 3 and
Phase 4.

We reiterated our prior year’s audit recommendation that the Municipal
Treasurer prepare and submit to the Municipal Accountant a monthly
Schedule of Unreleased Checks.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Prepare the necessary adjusting entries to derecognize the balance of
the closed Cash in Bank-Local Currency account in the SEF book
amounting to ₱77,206.74 and the negative balance of ₱68,485.93 in the
Trust Fund book;
b. Prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the unreleased
checks based on the schedule prepared by the Municipal Treasurer at
the end of every year to fairly present the Cash in Bank balance of the
LGU in the Financial Statements, and to reverse the entries every
January of the ensuing year; and
c. Effect immediately the corresponding adjustments pertaining to valid
reconciling items (unrecorded Debit and Credit Memos) amounting
₱166,550.00 that remained unadjusted as of year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Draw an adjusting entry to correct the misclassification of accounts in
the transfer of unexpended LDRRMF and to recognize the Due to Other
Funds and Due from Other Funds accounts in the General Fund and
Trust Fund books, respectively; and
b. Henceforth, strictly comply with the accounting and reporting
guidelines for LDRRM Funds of LGUs as enumerated in COA Circular
No. 2012-002 and record all the transactions of the municipality using
the appropriate accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts
for LGUs, so that transactions are faithfully and correctly presented in
the financial statements, which will provide reliable data to the users
thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare adjusting entries to
record the receipt of funds from the General Fund in the Trust Fund books of
account the amount of ₱2,274,999.00.

We also recommended that the Municipal DRRM Officer, together with the
Municipal Budget Officer and Municipal Accountant, ensure that the DRRM
Funds are indeed available or on hand before incurring any obligations or
disbursements thereof to make sure that funds are utilized for their intended
purpose/s.

We recommended that the Accountable Officers concerned submit within 30
days from receipt of the AOM, the liquidation reports and the supporting
documents, otherwise, restitute/return immediately the amount equivalent to
their respective unliquidated cash advances.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Stop the grant of cash advances to officials and employees with
outstanding cash advances; and
b. file the request for authority to write off the dormant unliquidated cash
advances to the COA Audit Team Leader (ATL) and/or Supervising
Auditor (SA) after exhausting all possible remedies by management to
demand liquidation of cash advances.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Demand from the accountable officers with unliquidated cash
advances, the immediate settlement, refund or submission of
liquidation reports on cash advances where the purposes have already
been served;
b. Prepare the schedule of dormant unliquidated cash advances by
accountable officer/debtor/government entity and by account, to
support the request for write-off, after exhaustion of all possible
remedies by the Management to demand liquidation of cash advances;
and
c. Discontinue processing DVs for grants of additional cash advance to
officials and employees with unliquidated previous cash advances to
avoid accumulation thereof.

We reiterated our prior year’s recommendation that the Mayor, as Chairman
of the Municipal Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and
Development Officer, identify and include in the AIP priority projects which
are ready for implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimal
utilization of the 20% DF.

We also reiterated our CY 2022 audit recommendations with modification that
the Municipal Engineer:
a. Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects prior to its inclusion in
the AIP which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates and plans/specifications;
b. Prepare the Program of Work with the Detailed Estimates and
plans/specifications for the unexpended balance of the Construction/
rehabilitation of potable water system amounting ₱5,946,755.79 to
ensure its full implementation; and
c. Submit to the Bids and Awards Committee the Program of Work with
the Detailed Estimates, plans/specifications and Purchase Request
within the timetable indicated in the AIP, taking into consideration the
period within which the procurement process and project
implementation will be completed.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and other concerned personnel
undertake measures to ensure the immediate utilization of the water reservoir
project in Barangay Obispo.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Municipal Engineer
render satisfactory explanation on approving the contractor’s requests for
work suspensions, variation order, and time extensions despite the noted
deficiencies in the attachments and supporting documents to validate the
claims indicated in the contractor’s requests, without prejudice to the issuance
of notice of suspension or disallowance for deficiencies found in audit.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor direct:
a.) the Municipal Engineer to strictly adhere to the project duration
stipulated per contract and exercise diligence in the inspection of
completed projects immediately after the reported date of completion
to determine whether projects were completed on time, taking
considerations on Variation Orders or Time Extension, if any, and
make sure that all materials and prescribed procedures conform with
the Program of Work, Detailed Estimates and Plan; and
b.) the Municipal Accountant to impose Liquidated Damages, equal to
one-tenth (1/10) of one (1%) of the cost of the unfinished portion of
the project for each day of delay.

Also, to protect the interest of the government and to prevent further loss and
wastage of government funds and resources, we recommend that the following
measures be undertaken for future projects of similar in nature:
a.) Strictly monitor implementation of all projects, whether contracts for
infrastructure, goods or consulting services, in accordance with the
approved plans and specifications within the time frame specified in the
contract agreement; and
b.) Identify contracts which require appropriate management actions. If
so, impose liquidated damages and/or administrative sanctions
particularly termination of contracts and recommend blacklisting of
contractors for failure to comply with their obligations in accordance
with the provisions of Sections 68 and 69, and Appendix 17 of the
Revised IRR of R.A. 9184.

We recommended that the Local Development Council re-appropriate the
balances of completed projects to other PPAs so that funds are optimally
utilized to help the Municipality achieve its desired socio-economic
development and environmental outcomes.

We recommended that the Municipal DRMM Officer:
a. Stop the distribution of relief goods to non-disaster victims and strictly
comply with the provisions of RA No. 10121 to avoid committing
prohibited acts in the implementation, distribution, and utilization of
resources out of the Disaster Risk Reduction and Management
(DRRM) Fund;
b. Determine the quantity of goods to be procured based on the number
of victims and their basic needs; and
c. Include as one of the conditions in the procurement of relief goods, the
expiration dates at least six months from the delivery date to avoid
spoilage or wastage of government funds.

We recommended that the Municipal DRMM Officer account for the missing
relief goods amounting to ₱65,939.87 and provide the Audit Team with the
distribution lists, if these were indeed distributed to the beneficiaries, and
henceforth strictly monitor the distribution of relief goods to avoid the
shortages in the future.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant prepare the necessary
accounting entries for the reversion of the CY 2017 unexpended LDRRMF
balance back to the unappropriated surplus of the General Fund.

We also recommended that the Municipal Disaster Risk Reduction and
Management Council (MDRRMC), through the MDRRM Officer, initiate the
reversion of the CY 2017 unexpended LDRRMF and identify the Social
Services Programs, Projects, and Activities (PPAs) to be funded from the
reverted funds and include the same in the LDRRMF Investment Plan.

We recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee, thru the BAC
Chairman:
a. Submit a justification why contracts were awarded to the above
suppliers and/or contractors despite the deficiencies noted;
b. Instruct the end users to refrain from indicating in the purchase
requests form and/or bidding documents the brand names of goods
which the LGU intends to procure, to promote competitiveness in all
its procurement activities; and

Henceforth, strictly comply with the provisions of the Revised IRR of RA 9184
to promote transparency and competitiveness and prevent the possible
invalidity of the resulting contracts.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
a. Ensure thru the Property/Supply Officer to accomplish the pre-repair
inspection report after the conduct of pre-repair inspection of
properties subjected for repairs. Likewise, when the subject properties
were already repaired, conduct immediately post-repair inspection and
submit a report thereon;
b. Direct the Property/Supply Officer to attach the Certificate of
Acceptance and Report of Waste Material duly approved by the LCE
after preliminary inspection/evaluation has been made;
c. Direct the Supply Officer to ensure that the damaged/replaced,
unserviceable supplies and property were kept in a safe stockroom
located within the premises of the Municipality waiting for disposal
thereof and disposed of in the manner prescribed by existing rules and
regulations; and
d. Require the Municipal Accountant to see to it that all claims for repairs
are supported with complete documentation to ensure that government
funds are expended for proper and valid expenses as require under
existing regulations.

We reiterated our audit recommendations in CY 2021 that the Municipal
Mayor create an Inventory Committee that will conduct the inventory taking
following the procedural guidelines provided in COA Circular 2020-006 dated
January 31, 2020.

We also reiterated our audit recommendations with modification, that the
Municipal Accountant:
a. maintain a complete and updated PPE and Real Property Ledger Cards to
ensure that all PPEs are properly accounted for and to easily facilitate the
reconciliation of the RPCPPE with accounting records;
b. Trace back the transactions in the PPE balance labelled as “for update”
from prior years to determine its nature and prepare the appropriate
adjustments in the books of the Municipality;
c. reclassify as inventories the items with acquisition costs that are below the
capitalization threshold of ₱15,000.00;
d. reclassify the incorrectly recorded PPEs to their proper account;
e. reconcile the PPELC with the RPCPPE, upon submission of the inventory
committee, wherein differences noted should be verified and the
corrections be made, where necessary; and
f. determine the cost of various donated equipment and recognize them as
PPE in the books.

Finally, we recommended that the Supply Officer:
a. Prepare an Inventory Custodian Slip for items below the capitalization
threshold in lieu of the previously issued Property Acknowledgment
Receipt; and
b. submit to the Municipal Accountant the documents of the donated
properties cited in paragraph number 1.5.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. conduct verification, analysis, and validation of the balances of the Due to
NGAs accounts, determine the source agencies and the status of programs,
projects and activities for which funds were received and maintain
complete records of the same;
b. exhaust possible remedy to reconcile the balances of the account and effect
the necessary adjustments, as warranted; and
c. make representations with identified source agencies for the return of
unutilized balances.

We also recommended that henceforth, the Municipal Mayor mandate the
LGU officials designated as Project-in-Charge to:
a. implement immediately the projects in accordance with the timeframe
provided for in the MOA to maximize the beneficial use of government
projects/program and the eventual return of unused funds, if any;
b. prepare the corresponding reports for each of the completed projects and
submit the same to the Accountant within one month from completion; and
c. report to the Municipal Mayor any project/program which cannot be
immediately implemented for proper disposition or referral to the Source
Agency/ies.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer furnish the Municipal
Accountant at the beginning of each year a duly certified list showing the name
of taxpayers and the amount due and collectible for the year which will serve
as basis of the latter in the booking up of the RPT and SET Receivable
accounts in the books.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer and Municipal
Accountant undertake periodic reconciliation of the balances per master list
of RPT delinquencies with that of the RPT and SET Receivable account
balances per books to come up with a more accurate and proper balance of
RPT and SET Receivable accounts.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant take up the
necessary adjusting/correcting entries in the books on the advance payments
of RPT and SET Receivables collected during 2022. Likewise, he should follow
the correct accounting entries regarding advance payments of real property
and special education taxes to reflect the correct amount of RPT and SET
Income, Due to LGU’s, and other affected accounts in the financial statements.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the agency:
a. require the designated Supply Officer to prepare the monthly report of fuel
consumption and to submit the reports to the Audit Team for audit
purposes; and
b. instruct all driver personnel of the municipality to accomplish the Driver’s
Trip Tickets and prepare the report of official travel on a monthly basis
and to submit the same to the Supply Officer for the preparation of the
monthly report of fuel consumption.

We also recommended that the BAC, thru the BAC Chairman, prepare the
Memorandum of Agreement indicating the Approved Budget of the Contract
for the fuel, oil, and lubricant to be procured to avoid incurrence of fuel
expenditures beyond the approved appropriation of the LGU.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant stop the practice of
recording immediately to expense accounts the inventories procured/ received by
the municipality. Recognize the corresponding expense account based on the
monthly Report of Supplies and Materials Issued, supported by duly approved RIS.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Record the cost of meals for seminars/workshop/trainings to the Training
Expense account and the cost of meals for meetings and other activities to
the Other Maintenance and Operating Expense account instead of the Food
Supplies Expense account pursuant to The Revised Chart of Accounts for
Local Government Units; and
b. Henceforth, record all the transactions of the municipality using the proper
accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs so that
transactions are faithfully and correctly presented in the financial statements
which will provide reliable data to the users thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement
of goods and services charged from the current year appropriation, the
obligations of the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on the
contract, during the current year, to facilitate the recording of the
expenses in the books of accounts of the municipality; and
b. Record only the accounts payable which were obligated and have
complete supporting documents.

We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer ensure the
forwarding of all unpaid valid claims to the Municipal Accountant’s Office for
proper recording of the payables and expenditures incurred during the year
in the book of accounts of the LGU.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Prepare the necessary journal entry to adjust the Government Equity and
Due from Local Government Units accounts of the municipality for the fund
transfers to barangays amounting ₱2.7 million that were not yet supported
with Liquidation Reports or Fund Utilization Reports; and
b. Henceforth, record all the transactions of the municipality using the
proper accounts provided in the Revised Chart of Accounts for LGUs so
that transactions are faithfully and correctly presented in the financial
statements which will provide reliable data to the users thereof.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer prepare and submit to the
Municipal Accountant a monthly Schedule of Unreleased Checks.

We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. To affect immediately the corresponding adjustments pertaining to valid
reconciling items (unrecorded Credit Memos) that remained unadjusted
as of year-end; and
b. Henceforth, prepare the Journal Entry Voucher (JEV) to restore the
unreleased checks based on the schedule prepared by the Municipal
Treasurer at the end of every year to fairly present the Cash in Bank
balance of the LGU in the Financial Statements, and to reverse the entries
every January of the ensuing year.

Further, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant and Municipal
Treasurer identify the reconciling items reflected in the SEF and Trust Fund
Bank Reconciliation Statements to afford accuracy and reliability of the
reported balances of the Cash in Bank accounts of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Mayor, as Chairman of the Municipal
Development Council, and the Municipal Planning and Development Officer,
identify and include in the AIP priority projects which are ready for
implementation in the current budget year to ensure optimal utilization of the
20% DF.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer:
a. Conduct a detailed engineering of all projects prior to its inclusion in the
AIP which includes the preparation of the Program of Work with the
Detailed Estimates and plans/specifications;
b. Fast-track the implementation of priority projects identified for CY 2022
and in the subsequent years; and
c. Submit to the Bids and Awards Committee the Program of Work with the
Detailed Estimates, plans/specifications and Purchase Request within the
timetable indicated in the AIP, taking into consideration the period within
which the procurement process and project implementation will be
completed.

We further recommended that the Bids and Awards Committee schedule the
procurement of projects in accordance with the timeframe specified in the AIP
to ensure completion of the projects before the end of the year.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the Municipal
Disaster Risk Reduction Management Council (MDRRMC), through the
MDRRM Officer:
a. prepare the LDRRMF Investment Plan with the PPAs therein stated in
specific terms and aligned with the NDRRM Plan; and
b. include in the annual MDRRMF Investment Plan the programs, projects
and activities to be funded from the prior years’ unexpended LDRRMF to
ensure that its utilization is well-planned and duly authorized by the
MDRRM Council.

We recommended that the Municipal Health Officer record all donations-in-
kind received by the RHU in the Summary/List of Donations Received,
Distributed and Balances, duly supported with the (1) Acknowledgment
Receipts, (2) proof of receipt by and distribution to the beneficiaries not
necessarily in the required form, and (3) the inventory of remaining
undistributed items, if any; and submit a copy of the same to the Supply
Officer and the Municipal Accountant.

We also recommended that the Supply Officer:
a. assist the Municipal Health Officer in the inspection and acceptance of all
donations-in-kind, and in the conduct of inventory of the remaining
undistributed items, if any; and
b. maintain copies of the Summary/List of Donations Received, Distributed
and Balances and its supporting documents and prepare and maintain a
Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM.

Finally, we recommended that the Municipal Accountant, based on the
Registry of Donated Relief Goods for DRRM, make a disclosure in the Notes to
FS as to the quantity received, issued and balance of all donation-in-kind
received by the Municipality, with categorization as to food items, medicines
and medical supplies, hygiene kits, and others.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor and Head of the Human
Resources Management Office:
a. issue written guidelines on the standard procedures to be followed in the
hiring of JOs to ensure professional and impartial selection process.
b. limit the hiring of JOs only to the most necessary and only to complement
the existing workforce of the LGU; and
c. require the COS to provide details of the actual duties/tasks accomplished
in the monthly accomplishment report to facilitate proper evaluation.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Require all End-User Units to submit all documents for the procurement
of goods charged from the current year appropriation, the obligations of
the suppliers having been duly fulfilled, based on the contract, during the
current year, to facilitate the recording of the expenses in the books of
accounts of the Municipality; and
b. Record all valid claims chargeable against the appropriations in the year
the expenses were incurred in the books of accounts of the Municipality
and recognize payables for all unpaid expenditures as of yearend.

We also recommended that the Municipal Budget Officer initiate the
enactment of an Ordinance to make available the unexpended appropriation
which were reverted to the surplus of the general fund should there still be
payment of prior year’s unpaid obligations not recorded as payables, to avoid
the erroneous charging of prior years’ expenditures to the current year’s
appropriation.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the
Sangguniang Bayan, adopt and implement policies or guidelines to rationalize
and regulate the provision and use of the load/communication allowance
taking into consideration the following:
a. The amount of cell card/load allowances to be granted in relation to the
available landline and internet connection of the LGU; and
b. The official/employee who should be entitled to it and the amount to be
granted should only be limited for his official phone calls.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor, as head of the municipality,
instruct all the department heads to discontinue the practice of distributing
the cell cards to Contract of Service and Job Order personnel to avoid
disallowances in audit.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
a. Require the submission of the lacking documents for the basketball
tournaments conducted by the municipality; and
b. Henceforth, ensure that all disbursements and transactions of the LGU be
supported with complete documentation in accordance with COA Circular
No. 2012-001 and Section 4 (6) of PD 1445.

We recommended that the BAC, through the BAC Chairman, strictly conduct
the procurement of goods and infrastructure projects in accordance with the
approved Annual Procurement Plan and avoid procurement of goods and
projects with reference to brand names in the future.

transfer the unexpended balance of the Local DRRM Fund to the Special Trust Fund account in the Trust Fund books and record the corresponding Trust Liability – DRRM account; and
Prepare the necessary Journal Entry to reclassify the unexpended balance from prior years from Due to NGA to Trust Liability – DRRM account.

record the deposit made by the Municipal Treasurer on December 4, 2019 in the amount of P116,728.06;
to prepare correcting entries on the over-recorded collection on April 20,2020 in the amount of P124,727.00; the erroneous adjustment on the unrecorded deposit in October 2019 in the amount of P23,660.00; and the erroneous entry on the liquidation of cash advance for travel of P2,160.00.
We also recommended Municipal Treasurer prepare adjusting entries on the Cash Book for Cash-Local Treasury and Cash Advances-Payroll as identified in the Table.  Henceforth, she is reminded to record the transactions in the cashbook as they occur or daily, and foot and close the books at the end of each month to determine actual monthly balances and to correct and adjust her records.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive direct the:
Municipal DRRM Officer to ensure inclusion of PPAs in the revised LDRRMFIP charged to the prior year’s unexpended balance;
Municipal Budget Officer to properly monitor all request for obligation as to the availability of appropriation and allotments in accordance with the Annual Budget and/or Supplemental Budget; and
Municipal Treasurer to verify cash availability related to LDRRM before the preparation and issuance of check.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor:
require the Municipal Accountant to submit justification why payment was made to these claims despite the deficient and lacking supporting documents.
direct the BAC Chairman and the BAC Secretariat to submit immediately the lacking contract documents within five (5) days from receipt of this AOM.  This is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted.
Moving forward, we recommend that the Bids and Awards Committee shall exert their best efforts that all supporting documents to the contracts are complete and compliant with the requirements of Section 3.1.2 of COA Circular No. 2009-001 dated February 12, 2009 and Section 17.1 of the RIRR of RA 9184 and submit to the Audit Team within five (5) days after perfection of the contract.

We recommended that the Municipal Engineer should ensure inclusion of Occupational Safety and Health as separate pay items in every infrastructure project implemented in compliance with DPWH D.O.  No. 56 s. 2005 dated April 20, 2005.
We also recommended that the compliance on Construction Safety and Health Program shall be required on all winning bidders and monitor the implementation of LGU projects that the workers are adequately protected against occupational risks.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant:
submit to the Audit Team the lacking documents and a written explanation why the disbursement vouchers were recommended for approval by the Municipal Mayor despite the deficiency; and
henceforth, review the completeness of the supporting documents of the disbursement vouchers before forwarding these to the Local Chief Executive for approval.
We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer review the completeness of the signatures on the disbursement voucher before a check is prepared and signed and demand from the suppliers the issuance of properly dated official receipts or sales invoice as acknowledgment of the payment to avoid suspension in audit.

We recommended that the Local Chief Executive require the Municipal Accountant and other officials and employees concerned to submit to the Audit Team the lacking appropriate documents presented in Table 1 within 5 days from receipt of this Report. This is without prejudice to the issuance of a Notice of Suspension/Disallowance, if warranted.
We also recommended that the Municipal Accountant submit explanation for the Audit Team’s evaluation why disbursement vouchers were approved for payment despite the lacking documents.  Henceforth, ensure that all disbursements should be supported with complete documentations in accordance with the provisions in PD 1445 and COA Circular 2012-001 dated June 14, 2012.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should prepare an adjusting entry to correct the balance of the Road Network account in the amount of ₱5,239,429.77.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant to prepare an adjusting entry to reclassify the procured dump truck amounting ₱2,998,500.00 from Motor Vehicle account into Construction and Heavy Equipment account.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should prepare a journal entry voucher to record the donated motor vehicle to its proper Property, Plant and Equipment (PPE) account and the corresponding depreciation of the asset pursuant to IPSAS 17

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should prepare a journal voucher to restore the amount in the books. Henceforth, he should always prepare the appropriate journal entries to record reconciling items to put the accounts in order.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should prepare an adjusting journal entry voucher on all relief goods or inventories that remained undistributed as at year-end.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should submit justification why the voucher was processed and endorsed for payment despite the defect of the contract and the OSS.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor (the HoPE) should require the contractor to submit a Warranty Security to cover the defects of the work from final acceptance to a period prescribed in the pertinent section of RA 9184

Henceforth, management should comply with pertinent requirements before payment is made to avoid audit suspensions.
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should submit the required procurement documents, if none, justification why disbursements were made despite the lacking documents.

We recommended that the Municipal Social Welfare Development Officer should maintain a database of the LGU which shall contain the socio-economic information of a family which can be easily accessed, managed and updated. A regularly updated database makes profiling of individuals or families easy, thus, the office can identify beneficiaries within a short notice.

We recommended that the MSWD Officer and the LDRRM Officer should conduct inspection of the items delivered to them before these should be received.  They should also see to it that stocks or inventories are properly and adequately safeguarded against damage or spoilage to avoid wastage of government resources.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant should submit the required documents, if none, a justification for approving the said transactions even without the required documents.

We also recommended that henceforth, the LDRRM Council should:

Initiate the immediate transfer of the unexpended balance of LDRRMF to the special trust fund account.

Include in the LDRRMFIP, under separate caption, the list of projects and activities charged under the unexpended LDRRMF from previous years.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor should inform the Supervising Auditor and Audit Team Leader within ten (10) days after the award of the infrastructure project or before the start of the program/activity that the appropriate signboards and/or public notices are already posted.

We also recommended that the Municipal Engineer should see to it that the provisions of COA Circular No. 2013-004 on the required specifications and information of the signboards or tarpaulins of the LGU’s programs, projects and activities to be displayed and posted in the site is strictly adhered to, to fully comply the full disclosure policy governing public interest.
Also, at the beginning of the year, he should submit to the Supervising Auditor and Audit Team Leader the list of all on-going government projects/programs and activities and those that are to be implemented during the year.  The list shall include the project name; implementing unit, office or division if it is not the agency as a whole; brief description of the PPA; contractor or suppler, if any; mode of procurement; funding source; cost or approved budget; project duration including start and completion dates; and location.

We recommended that the Municipal Development Council, through the Municipal Mayor as Chairman, shall include only in the Annual Investment Plan the projects which can readily be implemented by the LGU during the year, through proper scheduling and prioritization.

We also recommended that the BAC shall conduct the bidding of projects contained in the AIP in accordance with the timetable indicated therein.

We recommended further that the Municipal Engineer shall implement all the projects awarded during the year to ensure utilization of the 20% Development Fund.

observations.recommendation:
We recommended that the Municipal Accountant shall:
a) prepare an Adjusting Entry to correct the balances of Interest Payable
and Loans Payable account under Trust Fund account in the amount of
to ₱7,833,333.34 and ₱538,427.03, respectively; and
b) prepare an Adjusting Entry to record the related expenses on the
payment of loan amortization under General Fund account totaling to
₱1,802,779.98.

We recommended that in the subsequent preparation of Financial Statements,
the Municipal Accountant shall present the current portion of Loans Payable,
Interest Payable and Other Payable accounts as a separate line item under
Current Liabilities in the Statement of Financial Position for a better
understanding on the financial status of the Municipality and provide the
necessary information for budgetary purposes.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant shall:
a) prepare an Adjusting Entry to correct the balances of the Road Network
account and its Accumulated Depreciation account in the amount of
₱638,049.88 and ₱37,064.93, respectively; and
b) record depreciation of road network accounts correctly from its initial
recognition and subsequent years, including the accurate computation of
its estimated useful life without providing for any residual value.

We recommended that the Municipal Accountant shall: (1) prepare an
accounting entry to correct the balances of Due to BIR account of all funds
totaling to ₱202,461.92, and; (2) provide the Municipal Treasurer with the data
on the taxes withheld every end of the month.

We also recommended that the Municipal Treasurer shall compare monthly
the amounts generated in the Alpha List against the data on taxes withheld
provided by the Municipal Accountant before remittances are made to avoid
any variances.

We recommended that the Municipal Treasurer shall:
a) Prepare a Daily Cash Position Report, segregated by mandatory fund
(20% DF, LDRRMF, SK Fund, etc.) and submit the same to the Municipal
Mayor to inform him on the availability of cash to support checks that he
will sign;
b) Maintain a Logbook on estimated and actual collections and the
disbursements therefrom, to make sure that the total checks issued
monthly will not exceed fifty percent (50%) of the uncollected estimated
revenue accruing to the General Fund.

We also recommended that the Local Finance Committee shall convene at least
once a month to discuss and monitor whether the targeted income are attained
with actual receipts, and to adjust cash disbursement program for the
remaining months, when necessary, to prevent the incurrence of cash
overdraft.

We further recommended that the Municipal Accountant shall assist the
Municipal Treasurer in monitoring the Cash in Treasury and Cash in Bank
balances by tallying their respective records every end of the month to ensure
that the LGU does not incur any deficit and overdraft.

We likewise recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall require the officials
responsible for the bank charges to pay the amount of ₱160,600.00 and deposit
the amount bank into the General Fund of the Municipality.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall:
a) demand from the Contractor of Salintubig Project Phase III the refund
of overpayment of ₱540,816.38;
b) make a written representation with DILG to follow-up the request on the
release of the remaining ₱2.4 Million for Salintubig Project Phase III;
c) issue a resumption order to the contractor of Salintubig Project Phase IV
to complete the project, and impose liquidated damages, if necessary;
and
d) require the Municipal Accountant and the Municipal Treasurer to
monitor the utilization of funds transferred from different Source
Agencies to avoid misapplication of funds of one project to another.

We recommended that the Municipal Development Council, through the
Municipal Mayor as Chairman, shall include only in the Annual Investment
Plan the projects which can readily be implemented by the LGU during the
year, through proper scheduling and prioritization.

We also recommended that the BAC shall conduct the bidding of projects
contained in the AIP in accordance with the timetable indicated therein.

We recommended further that the Municipal Engineer shall implement all the
projects awarded during the year to ensure utilization of the 20% Development
Fund.

We recommended that the BAC Chairman and its Members shall: (1) review
the Approved Budget for the Contract meticulously prior to any procurement,
based on the Certificate of Availability of Funds issued by the Budget Officer,
and; (2) comply with all the procurement processes prescribed in RA No. 9184
including the conduct of Pre-procurement Conference whenever required.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall require the BAC to follow
the general policy on competitive bidding, prohibit the splitting of contracts
and resort only to alternative modes of procurement if the conditions are met,
as provided under Sections 48, 52, and 53.9 of the IRR of RA No. 9184.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall create the mandatory
positions, after considering the PS limitation of the LGU, of the three staff of
the LDRRMO, which may be augmented through any or a combination stated
in the Section 5.5 of NDRRMC-DILG-DBM-CSC Joint Memorandum Circular
2014-1.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall:
a) Require the Municipal Treasurer and the former Municipal Accountant-
designate to discuss and settle with the BIR the penalties and surcharges
imposed on the LGU totaling ₱223,616.28, without using government
funds; and
b) Require the Municipal Treasurer to monitor the cash balances of the
LGU and set aside the amount of taxes withheld so that remittances to
the BIR can be made within the reglementary period.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall revise the contents of the
Contract of Lease to conform with Municipal Ordinance No. 2014-02 and
propose amendments to update the rates and its provisions so that all
collections arising therefrom shall be binding and supportive of the LGU’s
goals.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall revise the Contract of
Lease, to be signed between renters of the stalls at the Calubian Sports
Complex and the LCE, to conform with Municipal Ordinance No. 2019-05
dated December 16, 2019 once the authority from the Sangguniang Bayan is
secured.

We recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall:
a) Initiate the procurement of a lot as the new dump site and develop therein
a sanitary landfill as a final garbage disposal site of the LGU, upon
securing permits and licenses from the DENR, in order to stop the
operation of an open dump site, and;
b) Implement the Safe Closure and Rehabilitation Plan on the dumpsite
located at Brgy. Tagharigue.

We recommended that Municipal Mayor, in coordination with the Solid Waste
Management Board, shall comply and integrate all the data needed by EMB
as listed in its Evaluation Report and resubmit its MSWMP to EMB for
endorsement approval by the National Solid Waste Management Commission.

We also recommended that the Municipal Mayor shall limit the number of JO
personnel only to the most urgent and necessary and to appropriate the budget
for JO wages from the programs and activities to which their services are
directly rendered. Wages of JO’s hired as laborers of a particular project
should be included in the cost of such project or program.

execute a Contract of Service with each Job Order personnel being hired, for
his signature, using the prescribed CSC format, stating therein specific work
assignment, the expected work to be accomplished, wages and other required
terms and conditions of the contracting parties.

c) Prioritize the provision of funds for the acquisition of additional
equipment such as bulldozer and garbage truck which will be used solely
for the implementation of solid waste management;
d) Assist the 50 barangays to establish their own Materials Recovery Facility
by providing financial assistance in order to facilitate the segregation of
solid waste at the barangay level, to have a proper solid waste management
and closely monitor their implementation; and
e) Lastly, fill in the item for MENRO who will be assigned as Solid Waste
Management Focal Person in order to ensure proper and effective
implementation of Solid Waste Management Plan.

We recommend that the Municipal Mayor require the created CLCPC
to perform actively their assigned functions and prepare annually the necessary
documents for submission to the Municipal Budget such as the LCPC Annual Work
and Financial Plan (AWFP) prioritized according to needs for integration in the local
development plan and other reports. We also recommended that the Municipal
Budget Officer allocate funds in its Annual Budget for the implementation of
programs identified by the CLCPC based on its AWFP.

We recommend that the Local School Board Council incorporate in its
budget programs and activities intended for Early Childhood Care Development.
